content.text: Demand Intake Portal and Triage Process

A demand intake portal is the front door for teams that want to build AI features using Claude. Without one, requests arrive via Slack messages, corridor conversations, and one-off emails — each handled differently, with no visibility into the pipeline, no consistent prioritisation, and no audit trail. A portal standardises how requests enter the system, automates initial scoring with Claude, and routes each request to the right reviewer on a defined SLA.

attrs.alt: Demand intake and triage flow: submission, auto-triage scoring, routing to fast track / standard queue / defer, then implementation

content.text: Why a Portal Matters at Enterprise Scale

When one team manages Claude API access for twenty product teams, ad hoc intake breaks down within the first month. The problems that emerge are predictable: high-impact requests sit unseen while low-value ones consume reviewer time; compliance risks surface after implementation rather than before; teams duplicate work because nobody knows what has already been built.
Problem (no portal)
Solution (with portal)
Requests arrive via Slack with no structure
Structured form captures all required information upfront
No visibility into the pipeline
Requesters can check status at any time via request ID
Triage is manual and inconsistent
Claude auto-scores every request within minutes of submission
High-value requests wait behind low-value ones
Composite score routes high-impact requests to fast track
Security and compliance review is an afterthought
PII flag and data sensitivity captured at intake; routed to compliance
Duplicated use cases across teams
Search on past requests before approving a new one
The Intake Form

The form has four sections: requester identity, use-case description, business case, and data classification. Every field that is optional in the form is required for fast-track routing — incomplete requests default to standard queue until the information is provided.

# intake_form.py
# Data model for the Claude AI use-case intake form.
# This form is typically rendered as a web form (Jira, ServiceNow, or custom).

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class UseCase(str, Enum):
    CUSTOMER_FACING   = "customer_facing"
    INTERNAL_TOOL     = "internal_tool"
    DATA_PIPELINE     = "data_pipeline"
    DEVELOPER_TOOLING = "developer_tooling"
    RESEARCH          = "research"
    OTHER             = "other"

class DataSensitivity(str, Enum):
    PUBLIC      = "public"
    INTERNAL    = "internal"
    CONFIDENTIAL = "confidential"
    RESTRICTED  = "restricted"

@dataclass
class IntakeRequest:
    # Who is requesting
    requester_name: str
    requester_email: str
    team: str
    department: str

    # What they want to build
    title: str                          # Short name, e.g. "Support ticket auto-classifier"
    description: str                    # 2-5 sentence description
    use_case_type: UseCase
    target_users: str                   # "Customer support agents (50 users)"
    expected_monthly_requests: int      # Rough volume estimate
    latency_requirement_ms: int         # Expected p95 latency budget

    # Business case
    problem_statement: str              # What problem does this solve?
    success_metric: str                 # How will you know it worked?
    estimated_monthly_value_usd: float  # Conservative revenue or cost saving
    expected_go_live: str               # "Q3 2026"

    # Data and compliance
    data_sensitivity: DataSensitivity
    pii_involved: bool
    external_data_sources: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    compliance_notes: str = ""

    # Optional: technical sketch
    proposed_model: Optional[str] = None   # "claude-haiku-4-5" or None if unknown
    caching_applicable: bool = False
    batch_applicable: bool = False

    # Auto-populated by portal
    request_id: str = ""
    submitted_at: str = ""
    status: str = "pending_triage"       # pending_triage | fast_track | standard | deferred | declined
    triage_score: Optional[float] = None
    assigned_reviewer: Optional[str] = None
Field guidance for submitters
expected_monthly_requests:
Order of magnitude is fine. 'Roughly 10,000/month' is better than leaving it blank. This drives the cost estimate that the CoE reviews.
estimated_monthly_value_usd:
Conservative is better than optimistic. A $50K/month estimate that proves correct builds trust. A $500K estimate that proves wrong undermines the program.
success_metric:
Must be measurable. 'Improve customer satisfaction' is not a metric. 'Reduce first-reply time from 4 hours to 2 hours for P1 tickets' is.
data_sensitivity + pii_involved:
The compliance routing depends entirely on these two fields. Misclassifying data sensitivity delays approval by 5–10 business days for a compliance audit.
Auto-Triage with Claude

As soon as a request is submitted, a background job sends it to Claude for scoring. The scorer evaluates two dimensions: impact (how much business value could this create?) and feasibility (how likely is this to succeed given the technical and compliance constraints?). The composite score determines routing.

# auto_triage.py
# Score incoming requests automatically using Claude.
# Produces a numeric score (1-10) and a structured triage recommendation.

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class TriageResult:
    request_id: str
    impact_score: float        # 1-10: business value potential
    feasibility_score: float   # 1-10: technical and compliance feasibility
    composite_score: float     # weighted: 0.6*impact + 0.4*feasibility
    routing: str               # "fast_track" | "standard" | "deferred" | "declined"
    key_risks: list[str]
    recommended_reviewer: str  # role: "CoE engineer" | "compliance" | "product manager"
    rationale: str


TRIAGE_SYSTEM = """You are a Claude AI program intake coordinator.
Score each request on impact (1-10) and feasibility (1-10).
Impact: expected business value, number of users affected, strategic alignment.
Feasibility: technical complexity, data sensitivity, compliance risk, timeline.

Routing rules:
- composite >= 7.5: fast_track (CoE review in 3 days)
- composite 5-7.4: standard (next sprint)
- composite 3-4.9: deferred (revisit next quarter)
- composite < 3: declined (not viable as described)

Output JSON matching this schema:
{
  "impact_score": float,
  "feasibility_score": float,
  "composite_score": float,
  "routing": "fast_track|standard|deferred|declined",
  "key_risks": ["string"],
  "recommended_reviewer": "string",
  "rationale": "string (2 sentences)"
}"""


def triage_request(request: dict) -> TriageResult:
    """
    Auto-triage an intake request. `request` is a dict of IntakeRequest fields.
    Returns a TriageResult with scores and routing decision.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=TRIAGE_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Score this Claude AI request:\n{json.dumps(request, indent=2)}",
        }],
    )

    result = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return TriageResult(
        request_id=request.get("request_id", ""),
        impact_score=result["impact_score"],
        feasibility_score=result["feasibility_score"],
        composite_score=result["composite_score"],
        routing=result["routing"],
        key_risks=result["key_risks"],
        recommended_reviewer=result["recommended_reviewer"],
        rationale=result["rationale"],
    )
Composite score
Routing
SLA
Who reviews
Fast track
CoE review within 3 business days
Senior CoE engineer + product manager
Standard queue
Sprint planning within 14 days
CoE engineer
Deferred
Revisit next quarter
CoE lead (quarterly triage meeting)
Declined
Requester notified within 2 days
Auto-decline with rationale

Auto-triage is a first pass, not a final decision. The CoE engineer reviewing a fast-track request can override the score after reading the full context. The score's value is in routing — ensuring that a 9.2-score request does not sit in the same queue as a 3.1-score request for two weeks.
Portal Backend Implementation

The portal backend is a thin API layer that persists intake requests, triggers triage, and exposes a status endpoint. In production environments this typically integrates with an existing ticketing system (Jira, ServiceNow) rather than a custom store. The code below shows the core logic that any integration must implement.

# portal_api.py
# Minimal FastAPI backend for the demand intake portal.
# In production this integrates with Jira/ServiceNow; this shows the core logic.

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
from pydantic import BaseModel, EmailStr
import uuid, datetime, json
from pathlib import Path

app = FastAPI(title="Claude AI Intake Portal")

# In production: replace with a real database
REQUESTS_FILE = Path("intake_requests.jsonl")

class IntakeSubmission(BaseModel):
    requester_name: str
    requester_email: EmailStr
    team: str
    title: str
    description: str
    use_case_type: str
    expected_monthly_requests: int
    latency_requirement_ms: int
    problem_statement: str
    success_metric: str
    estimated_monthly_value_usd: float
    data_sensitivity: str
    pii_involved: bool

@app.post("/intake/submit")
async def submit_request(
    submission: IntakeSubmission,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
):
    request_id = f"AI-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8].upper()}"
    record = {
        "request_id": request_id,
        "submitted_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "status": "pending_triage",
        **submission.model_dump(),
    }
    # Persist immediately
    with REQUESTS_FILE.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

    # Triage runs in the background so the submitter gets an instant response
    background_tasks.add_task(run_triage, record)

    return {
        "request_id": request_id,
        "message": f"Request {request_id} received. You will receive a triage decision within 1 business day.",
        "status_url": f"/intake/status/{request_id}",
    }

@app.get("/intake/status/{request_id}")
async def get_status(request_id: str):
    if not REQUESTS_FILE.exists():
        raise HTTPException(404, "No requests found")
    for line in REQUESTS_FILE.read_text().splitlines():
        rec = json.loads(line)
        if rec["request_id"] == request_id:
            return {
                "request_id": request_id,
                "status": rec.get("status"),
                "triage_score": rec.get("triage_score"),
                "routing": rec.get("routing"),
                "rationale": rec.get("rationale"),
            }
    raise HTTPException(404, f"Request {request_id} not found")

async def run_triage(record: dict) -> None:
    """Background task: score the request and update status."""
    from auto_triage import triage_request
    try:
        result = triage_request(record)
        record.update({
            "status": result.routing,
            "triage_score": result.composite_score,
            "routing": result.routing,
            "rationale": result.rationale,
            "key_risks": result.key_risks,
            "recommended_reviewer": result.recommended_reviewer,
        })
        # Rewrite the record in the file (simplified; use DB upsert in production)
        lines = REQUESTS_FILE.read_text().splitlines()
        updated = []
        for line in lines:
            rec = json.loads(line)
            if rec["request_id"] == record["request_id"]:
                updated.append(json.dumps(record))
            else:
                updated.append(line)
        REQUESTS_FILE.write_text("\n".join(updated) + "\n")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Triage failed for {record['request_id']}: {e}")
Integration notes
If using Jira: create a custom issue type 'Claude AI Request' with fields matching IntakeSubmission. The auto-triage job runs as a Jira automation or webhook.
If using ServiceNow: map the intake form to a Request Item. The triage score populates a custom field; the routing populates the Assignment Group.
For small programs (< 5 teams): a shared Notion form with a Zapier automation to run the triage script is a viable starting point.
Always persist requests before running triage. If the triage job fails, the request is not lost.
SLA Monitoring

SLA breaches are the most common failure mode of intake programs. A request that enters 'fast track' and sits unreviewed for two weeks is worse than no portal — the team has been given an expectation that was then violated. An SLA monitor runs daily and alerts the CoE lead to overdue items.

# sla_monitor.py
# Alert when requests breach their SLA for triage or review completion.

import datetime
import json
from pathlib import Path

SLA_HOURS = {
    "fast_track": 72,   # 3 business days
    "standard": 336,    # 2 sprints = 14 calendar days
    "deferred": 720,    # 30 days
    "pending_triage": 24,  # auto-triage should run within 1 day
}

def check_sla_breaches(requests_file: str) -> list[dict]:
    now = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
    breaches = []
    for line in Path(requests_file).read_text().splitlines():
        rec = json.loads(line)
        submitted = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(rec["submitted_at"])
        status = rec.get("status", "pending_triage")
        if status in ("declined", "implemented", "closed"):
            continue
        sla_hours = SLA_HOURS.get(status, 24)
        elapsed = (now - submitted).total_seconds() / 3600
        if elapsed > sla_hours:
            breaches.append({
                "request_id": rec["request_id"],
                "title": rec.get("title", ""),
                "team": rec.get("team", ""),
                "status": status,
                "sla_hours": sla_hours,
                "elapsed_hours": round(elapsed, 1),
                "overdue_by_hours": round(elapsed - sla_hours, 1),
            })
    return sorted(breaches, key=lambda x: -x["overdue_by_hours"])
Run the SLA monitor as a daily cron job. Post results to the FinOps Slack channel.
Escalate to the CoE lead when a fast-track request is more than 24 hours overdue.
Report SLA breach rate (breaches / total requests) in the monthly FinOps KPI report as an operational health metric.
A breach rate above 15% indicates the CoE does not have enough reviewer capacity. Escalate to engineering management.
Triage Meeting for Edge Cases

The auto-triage handles 80% of requests cleanly. The remaining 20% are edge cases: requests that score in the 4–6 range where the routing is genuinely ambiguous, requests that involve novel compliance questions, or requests from teams whose value estimates are unreliable. A weekly 30-minute triage meeting reviews these cases and makes a human routing decision.
Meeting input
Meeting output
All requests with composite score 4.5–6.5
Manual routing override with rationale logged
Requests flagged by auto-triage as compliance-sensitive
Referred to compliance team or cleared
Requests deferred > 90 days
Close or escalate to next quarter
Declined requests with an appeal from the requester
Reviewed by CoE lead; final decision recorded
Summary

A demand intake portal for Claude AI requests standardises submission, automates initial scoring, and routes each request to the appropriate reviewer on a defined SLA. The intake form captures the business case, technical context, and data classification at submission time rather than during review. Claude's auto-triage scores impact and feasibility within minutes and routes high-composite requests to a 3-day fast track. SLA monitoring prevents requests from stalling invisibly. A weekly triage meeting handles the edge cases that fall between routing thresholds. Together these components give the CoE a manageable, auditable pipeline for the full enterprise demand for AI features.
